name: ai-attachment-picker
title: AI Attachment Picker
description: Attachment entry control that offers caller-defined attachment types with file-picker and capture affordances, states the PHI and sharing constraints inline, and hands the selected files straight back to the caller.

props.name: types
props.description: The attachment types this product allows: { id, label, description?, icon?, accept?, multiple?, capture?, disabled? }.

props.name: restrictions
props.description: Required. Allowed formats, PHI handling, and external-sharing limits, in the caller's words — rendered inside the popover.

props.name: onSelect
props.description: Fires with the chosen files and the id of the type they came from. The caller owns everything after this.

props.name: limitLabel
props.description: Size and format limit copy, e.g. "PDF or JPEG · up to 20 MB".

clinicalNotes: The moment a file leaves a clinician's device is the moment PHI moves, and by then it is too late to explain the rules. So `restrictions` is required and rendered at the point of decision, above the file browser rather than behind a terms link: say what may be attached, who will be able to see it, and whether anything leaves your boundary. The commonest real-world harm here is not a hostile actor but a misfile — the wrong patient's discharge summary dragged into an open encounter — which is why types carry caller-authored descriptions and the selected files come back tagged with the type id so the caller can route and audit them. The component holds no bytes: it opens a picker, hands you `File[]`, and makes no assumption about storage, retention, or de-identification. Uploading, virus scanning, consent, and audit logging remain entirely yours.
notes: One hidden file input, configured imperatively per type (accept/multiple/capture) because a state update would not land before .click(). `capture` maps to capture="environment" — a hint only; desktop browsers fall back to the file browser.
